speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1996. I'm interviewing Eva Lax Slomovits. And this is tape number six of our interview. When you finished the last tape, you were discussing this incident where this boy-- this person who wouldn't beat the girls and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a Polish Jewish boy. And he was a kapo. And he was assigned to, to the Strafkommando. And this-- the commandant watched him that he's not doing his job well. So he let the dogs go on him. And the dogs bit him till he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died on the spot, or he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was bleeding there, there. And then they took him away. But we heard after that he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have contact with the commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only [BACKGROUND NOISE] only that time. Somebody is ringing my bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we were Plaszow-- we came, we came there before, before [INAUDIBLE]. And we were there til September, beginning of-- end of August, beginning of September we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there through the whole summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole summer, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the conditions stay the same, or did they get worse? How do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it about-- no, it got better. It got better, because we, we were assigned to work already. We weren't anymore in Strafkommando. We were at work. So our job was-- they used to bring from the front soldiers-- and they would-- from, not soldiers-- from the soldiers, the clothes. And we would wash it and mend it and, and packed it in packages again. And the shoes were, were fix. And they send it probably back to the front. I don't know. But we had already more normal jobs. We worked in a building, so things got better. And even if we worked outside, we were already not like a Strafkommando. We were just building things or, or doing certain things. But the building was very difficult, because we used to, we used to dig the grounds and find the bodies. Because it was a cemetery. And every time we opened a grave-- you know, dug out a body or-- no matter how much I saw killings, I still was like shivering, to-- so and I would say, please forgive me. It's not my doing. When we op-- we, a body, we would find a body, we would apologize to the body. And we would try to hide it again in sand and cover it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know if these were-- was it a Jewish cemetery or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish cemetery, because the, the stones were-- the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The stones were on, on the floor. They made steps from the stones. It was a Jewish cemetery in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you informed that you were going to be leaving Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came out to the [? say ?] appell. The called roll call [? say ?] appell. And they said we should march. And we start marching. We don't know where we going again. And we marched through the gate. We knew that we, we're leaving Plaszow. But we didn't-- we didn't have any belongings or nothing. So we just marched out from there. And there was a-- not far, a train station. Many times we would go to work on this train station. They would deliver things. So we would go to the train station, take it off, and put a-- carry it into the camp, like bread, or, or products for the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the train station. And they start putting us on the cattle cars again. So they put in more-- they put a man, sick men from the hospital. They brought men. And they put in this sick men into the train. And they pushed and pushed more, more people. It was so crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming to, to-- from Auschwitz to Krakow, it took three hours. And going there, it took us two days to co-- go. The train was going back and forth. I guess they were bombed. So they didn't have-- though they were standing here. And they're going back and standing. And nobody told us where we going, what we doing. But when we ca-- we arrived to Auschwitz, we saw Auschwitz again. Now, we were already familiar with the place. We had some bread with us, because we-- in the morning, before the [? say ?] appell, we got the bread. So we had the bread. But we couldn't eat it, because we didn't have any water. So all we wanted is water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got off the train, there was like-- in Auschwitz-- like polluted water. And we start running to that water. And they start shooting people. But we didn't care. We ran anyhow. Then they stopped shooting. A few people were killed. But we, we still went to the water. And that water was polluted, so we blew up. It, It seems that it didn't agree with us. So we got undressed again. And we came back in front of Mengele. The first time, we didn't know about Mengele, because we never heard of Auschwitz. We never heard of selections. We didn't-- But the second time when we came there, we heard already about Mengele and the selections. So when we came in front Mengele, we were undressed. And he saw big stomachs, so he says to us in Hungarian-- he spoke Hungarian, Mengele-- he says, are you pregnant? We says, no, no, no. We're not pregnant. We-- so he let us go. We, we went this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then they took us in front of the crematorium. And we knew that we going to the crematorium. And they put us naked if-- in, in a like a big stable was there. And we were there a whole night. But that time, they brought in from Lodz-- this was in September, end of August, I think, beginning of September-- they brought in from Lodz ghetto trains. And they didn't even select them. They put-- took them all to, to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I guess we were waiting the crematorium should ease up, they should take us. And they said that we going to the crematorium. But after staying there for a whole night and a whole day, they decided to let us-- take us back to the barracks, so another miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us-- they took us to a shower. They gave us a shower. They gave us again clothes. And we went back to the-- to Auschwitz-- I mean, to Birkenau and the Czech block. And we were in 20-- block 29. It was-- the barrack was called the block.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a few days staying there, we didn't do any work there. We just stayed there in the morning roll call and at night roll call. And if was raining or-- so after a few days we were there, they gave us the number. They tattooed. So my number is A-8650. Would you be-- would you show us your number? Take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew what, what the, the date was, because we filled out cards. When, when they, when they tattooed us, they filled out cards. So it was September the third when they tattooed us. And-- I can't open.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can't do it--

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow-- here. A-8650. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So, they, they said that-- people were talking. You know, people were talking there amongst themselves. They says that this-- it's an advantage to have a number. Because these will be working people. If you have a number, they'll take you to work. But there were always selections. And they said that Auschwitz is a place where either you go the crematorium or you go to work. So if there are selections, Try To go to work. Because if not, when the crematorium is going to be empty, they going to-- you going to go to the crematorium. So every time there was a selection, we try to go to be selected to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we saw some civilian people came to select. Hey, it wasn't our choice. But they took us out to roll call. And they came. And they selected the people. So they, they selected. They-- we had to get undressed, completely naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to show our hands. And they looked at our eyes. And for some reason, I don't-- we don't know what, what for, they, they pick us. But they made a selection of 400 girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they picked always the stronger ones. So they picked my two sisters. And they didn't pick me. And we had decided that even we have to pay with our life, we have to stay together. If we going to stay together and help one another, we have a better chance. Because at Plaszow, they-- my sisters also helped me. If they wouldn't have been there, I don't know if I would have make it. So when they selected, the Naz-- the Nazi was walking. And I was barefoot. And there was sand. I was walking right behind him. And I ran to my sisters. So I says, thank God. I'm already again with my sisters. So they took us. They bathed us. And they gave us different, like civilian clothes-- a skirt, and a blouse, and a slip, and underwear, and a sweater. And I-- we felt it must be very important. Because for the first time, they're giving us this kind of clothes, civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and they gave us a half a bread. They gave us marmalade and a piece of, of salami. Says, gee, this is, this is wonderful. And we didn't know where we going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us on a train and-- still a cattle car train, but it wasn't crowded. There was maybe about 100 girls on the train. And we knew that we go someplace to work, because the way they selected us. We just didn't know what kind of work we're going to do. So we came-- the station said Neustadt. And we found out this is Neustadt, Oberschlesien. And they-- we walked from the train to a, a big building. And on the building, it says Frankel Simon, the name was. And we didn't know much about it. But later on, we found out that there was a Jewish person owned that factory. And before the war, he went to America. I wonder where he is now. So we still didn't know what kind of work we going to do. So some civilian people came into the, this-- where we stayed. There, there was like two floors. They divided 200 girls on each floor. They had beds like for soldiers, two stories. And everybody had already his own little bed, you know, two-- like a-- A bunk bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunk bed, so-- and a blanket. And, and they had a wash room there and a bathroom and was really something special, you know, after going through all these things without. So they came-- civilian people came. And they taught us how to make a knot with, with cotton. And we couldn't figure out this-- it was a different way how to knot the cotton together. And then the next day, they took us to a factory. And this was a weaving factory where they made fabrics for the parachutes, white fabric. So the fa-- the material was very thin. The cotton was very thin. So then we found out that you have to have good hands and good eyes. And for some reason, how much they looked, there were two girls that didn't have their fingers. One didn't have two fingers. One didn't have three fingers. And it was terrible, because they were going to take them back to Auschwitz. My sister, Lilly, was very beautiful girl all the time-- tall, slim. And she, she was a very beautiful girl. And I guess the Germans just picked, you know, the stronger girls. And they were in charge on the, the-- she didn't go to the factory to work. She was in charge to keep the place clean. And she had about six girls to help her to clean up the, the washroom. We had to make our own beds, but to clean up the floors and the washroom. Then we had a kitchen. Some girls worked in the kitchen. So my sister was in charge. And she was called the blockalteste.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when this girl, one of the girls didn't have the fingers-- so that she was with two other sisters. And they worked in the kitchen. So they came to my sister, Lilly. And they says, you have to do something. Because they want to take my sister, [INAUDIBLE], back to Auschwitz. So my sister Lilly says, what can I do? I, I, I'm just a like you are, a prisoner. She says, no, you have to go speak for my, my sister. She says, you have to tell them to either let her work in the kitchen or work in the-- to clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister says-- she says, you don't have a choice. You're the only one that could go and speak to the, the oberscharfuhrer. He was in charge of the camp. And til this day, I think he was a decent man, this oberscharfuhrer that was in charge, the main. He was an older person. He was maybe about 55 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked the SS that were guard there that she has to go speak to the oberscharfuhrer. They looked at her. How could you request something like that? You're a prisoner. You have no right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I must speak to the oberscharfuhrer. If you're not going to let me go, take me there. Because they lived a little bit further out. She says, if you're not going to take me, I'm going to complain to the oberscharfuhrer. Because she, she was in touch more with the SS. They, they used to come to inspect. So they knew her. They called her by the name, Lilly. She says, I'm going to complain to the oberscharfuhrer that I have a compliant. And you don't let me go to him. So she went to this oberscharfuhrer. And she to the ober-- asked the oberscharfuhrer if he would let this girl come and do the work, the cleaning, and, and take somebody else to go to the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he looked at her. And he says to her, you have a lot of guts. But he says, you know, I see that you're smart, too. I'll grant you your wish. But you have to promise me if the barracks is not going to be clean, that you're going to go to Auschwitz, too, with her. So my sister says, yes, I promise. And he let this girl go to work for my sister, clean. But we were so scared that my sister will pay with her life that we used to get up early in the morning to help to clean the, the washroom and the things. Because-- and her, her sisters, too, because they shouldn't take them away to Auschwitz. And the hou-- the place was spotless. Our barrack was always spotless, because we were scared. So we start working in this factory in Oberschlesien. And we worked there. This was really our best place, because we had decent food. We had clothes. We had wash.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked 12 hours, so 12 hours was two shifts. We worked 12 hours, and 12 hours we were home-- I mean, in the barrack. But we, we start getting to ourselves. You know, we ate. We were young. And the work, it was hard. Some-- once in awhile, there were young SS there, like the blockfuhrer, that if the oberscharfuhrer wasn't there, he would punish the girls. He would hit us [INAUDIBLE]. But we always knew that the oberscharfuhrer is not there, he is-- would, would have kill us. But the oberscharfuhrer, I think, was a very decent person, even he was a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Neustadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there till January 19th exactly. Because I, I know the 20th was my sister's, Lilly's, birthday. And she says, what a present I'm getting. We're leaving this, this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the oberscharfuhrer came. And he says to, to us-- he opened up the kitchen. And he opened up the magazine. And he says, take wool clothes. Take food, how much you can take it with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know where we going. And I don't-- I just have an order to take you away from here. But where we going to go, I'm going to-- on daily basis, they're going to tell me where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we left early in the morning. January 19th, we left. And we walked a whole day. And it was so bitter cold. But we had still good clothes. And we had food. And we walked. We came late at night. And he says, go to sleep. Say, how can we go to sleep on the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a, a big, like with a hay. He took out his-- the, you know, the soldiers carry this-- and he cut open the hay. And he threw it. And he says, lay down on the hay and dig in in the hay to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 400 girls. So it was so cold. But we fell asleep, because we were exhausted tired. And then next morning, again we walked. We walked-- and it seems that not only we walked, a lot of Germans walked. I don't know where they walked, but we saw a lot of have-- prisoners, the Jewish girls with striped clothes already dead on the road. I says, oh, my God. Where they walking? Where they going to walk us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked day and night. Day and night, we walked. And it was so terrible. We used to stop off sometimes in a, in a-- on a farm. And the farmers used to bring out hot coffee for the soldiers. They wouldn't give us nothing. It was snow that we used to eat, the snow from the blankets. And all of a sudden, the shoes already-- we didn't have shoes. So we saw dead girls on the road. We, we took off their shoes, their blankets. And we would put them on. And I apologized to them. I says, you don't need it anymore. You're not cold. I, I wish on myself that I, I shouldn't be cold anymore, and I shouldn't, I shouldn't need it. It was so horrible, the walking to see all these dead girls. And it was winter, so they were all frozen. And I never touched a dead person, but then now, I wasn't afraid anymore. I was, I was not like human.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked this day and night. One night, we slept in a, in a barn. There were cows or horses. And it seems that they, they didn't move. And it was so good, warm in there, because it was a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans-- we, we passed by Hungarian soldiers were marching also. And we told them in Hungarian that we also Hungarian. They should help us. They should give us some bread. And they said, you're not Hungarian. You're Jewish. If you would be Hungarian, you would be in Hungary. And they didn't give us any, any food. They didn't help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we marched for about, about 10 days-- two weeks, I think. And then we came to Gross-Rosen, to a camp, Gross-Rosen. And we-- they led us into this camp. We were just so over-tired. And there were men. And they put us on another side. And we asked these men from where they are. And they said they're Czech. I says, are Jewish? We asked it they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They says, no, we're not Jewish. We the Czech flyer-- pilots. And he says that we-- they-- when Czechoslovakia was overrun by the Nazis, they took us to camp. So they were there in Gross-Rosen. And they want to know how things are in Czechoslovakia. So I says-- we told them that we are already away from home also, almost a year. But we told them there is no more Czechoslovakia. We told them what's going on. And they brought us coffee. The-- and they were taking off their scarves, their sweaters. And they were giving us-- this men. And they were so happy to see Czech women, because they haven't seen them since, since they were in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they only want to know what's going on home. But we didn't know much to tell them about home. So this was-- we were there Gross-Rosen only about three days for this. Do you remember anything about the camp itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp-- I don't, don't know much, except we, we-- they didn't even take us out to roll call there. Because I guess it was just a stopover for us. And from there, the-- because there weren't any women there. There were-- we were the only women, 400 girls, that they took us in from Neustadt. They took us in there. But we were just there for a while, for about two, three days. And then after that, they told us again to march. But we didn't-- we only marched to the train. And they put us again in open cars. And we went-- we were going. Again, we never knew where, where we're going to wind up. So in Weimar, near Mauthausen, they bombed the sta-- we were on the station in Weimar. And they bombed the station. And the, the train turned over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the train turned over, the girls start running to, to, to look for-- we didn't know that in the back of the train, there are men. So they start running to find out, where are you from? Where are you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the nurse that was in Neustadt-- we had a nurse-- she found her husband there. And they left. They ran away. We found out after the war that they ran away from there. And a lot of girls were bombed. And, and they lost a limb, a hand, a foot. They were bleeding. And they would go to the soldiers and say, please just shoot me. They were in such a pain. They said, your friends did it. Your American friends did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- it was just a terrible sight. And we were saved because our train, when, when turned over, we wound up underneath. So from the bombs, the pieces didn't, didn't hurt us. So we sisters were saved again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the bombing was over, they turned over the train again. And we went back into the train. And we moved on. And we came to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop here to change tapes.
